methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $42M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Sunflower Labs, a Series B autonomous drone company founded by Alex Pachikov, presents a valuation challenge typical of early-stage frontier tech with sparse public comparables. The Drones & Autonomous sector comprises 8 private peers and no public companies, including players like Joby Aviation, Archer Aviation, and Zipline at various stages. This thin peer set constrains methodological diversity for valuation triangulation.

Given Sunflower Labs' $41.7M in total raised capital, the Stage-Progression method applies a 1.9x multiplier typical of Series B-to-growth stage step-ups, yielding $79M as the central estimate. The Peer-Median approach was not applicable; only one same-stage comparable exists within the narrow drone-autonomous filter, falling short of the three-datapoint threshold required for reliable median derivation. Attempting to broaden to all-sector peers would introduce material sector heterogeneity and misrepresent valuation drivers specific to autonomous hardware.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $67M (low) to $91M (high), centered on $79M, derived solely from stage-progression mechanics. Material uncertainties include the absence of adjacent-stage comparables to validate step-up assumptions, limited visibility into Sunflower Labs' unit economics or revenue traction, and the nascent maturity of autonomous drone markets themselves. A future series round or public listing of peer companies would substantially improve precision. Until then, any valuation outside the $67M–$91M band requires explicit assumptions beyond the provided data.
